data.title: AT&T: Why I'm Still Buying The High-Yielding Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

The share price of AT&T (NYSE:T) has been under pressure lately as the telecom sector in the United States is changing. In this excellent preview by fellow author Vladimir Dimitrov, the key elements to look out for in next week’s financial reports are clearly outlined. That being said, I remain a big fan of the fixed-income securities that have been issued by AT&T, as there are two issues of preferred stock outstanding, as well as a long-term debt security. And although I'm obviously also still waiting to see the actual results of the third quarter (out next week), I feel very confident in picking up both types of securities, which offer yields in excess of 6%, now.

A very robust result means the preferred dividends are well covered

As I'm mainly looking at AT&T from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, I'm of course very interested in seeing how well the preferred dividends are covered by the net profits and the cash flows.

While the company still has to report on its Q3, I'm not expecting a whole lot of surprises compared to the first two quarters of the current financial year. And looking back at the results of the second quarter of this year, we see the company reported total revenue of approximately $30.8B, which represents an increase of just over 3% compared to the second quarter of last year. Meanwhile, the operating expenses increased by less than 2%, which indeed means that the operating income jumped by almost 15%. Keep in mind the second quarter of 2024 also contained some non-recurring items like the $480 million impairment and restructuring charge. This indeed also means that on an underlying basis the operating expenses increased in 2025, but the financial performance remains very robust.

As the income statement above also shows, AT&T reported a pre-tax income of approximately $6.1B, which resulted in a net profit of $4.86B, of which $4.5B was attributable to AT&T. The income statement also shows there was a 36 million preferred stock dividend and redemption gain, which resulted in an underlying net profit of just under $4.5B for an EPS of $0.62. And looking at the performance in the entire first semester, the EPS was $1.22.

As you can notice in the image, the company combines the actual cost of the preferred dividends with the redemption gain related to buying back preferred stock on the open market at the discount to the principal value. During the first half of the year, the company repurchased just over $2 billion in preferred equity. In any case, the income statement shown above clearly shows the preferred dividends enjoy absolutely excellent coverage, as the company generally needs a low-single-digit percentage of its net profit to cover the preferred share dividends.

The risk/reward ratio of the preferred stock remains appealing

AT&T currently has two series of preferred shares outstanding, and the current yield of both series is approximately the same right now. I'm focusing on the Series C preferred stock (T.PR.C) which has the lowest preferred dividend coupon of 4.7%. As the current yield is the same as the Series A preferred shares, I tend to focus on the preferred stock that's trading at a higher discount to the principal value, as in this case it means the call risk is lower than higher yielding securities.

As you can see below, the series C cumulative preferred shares issued by AT&T are currently trading at $19.60. This means the current preferred different yield is approximately 6%.

Considering the risk free interest rate is approximately 3.59% for the US 5Y Treasury and 4.00% for the US 10-year Treasury, I think the markup of 220 bps based on the midpoint of the 5-year and 10-year Treasury rates is quite attractive.

Investment thesis

I have started to build a long position in both series of the preferred shares issued by AT&T, as well as the baby bonds that are also publicly tradeable. I like the risk/reward ratio offered by these preferred and debt securities despite the recently announced multi-billion-dollar deal to acquire spectrum licenses from Echostar for $23B. While that will have an initial negative impact on the balance sheet, it will allow AT&T to pursue additional opportunities.

Meanwhile, I continue to write (out of the money) put options on the common shares, as I would still be interested in initiating a long position.

data.title: Plains All American: The Market Is Sleeping On This High Yield Gem
data.description:
Plains All American Pipeline (NASDAQ:PAA) is an $11 billion market cap midstream partnership, which is a direct peer to such MLP gems as Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), MPLX LP (MPLX), and Western Midstream (WES).

PAA has also been one of my favorite MLP picks since April, 2024, when I published a rather bullish case on PAA's distribution offering, which, at that time, was certainly, in my view, the best in the entire MLP (AMLP) universe. PAA offered a FWD yield of 9%, which was a) backed by 190% cash flow coverage and b) supported by the management's commitment to increase it until the coverage metric normalizes at 160%. So, it was a de-risked high-yield play, which has so far worked out nicely. The quarterly distributions have increased from $0.32 per unit to $0.38 per unit, and, as I will elaborate below, we have a strong basis to count on additional hikes.

However, when speaking of income and its growth, it is always very important to pay attention to the underlying fundamentals and cash flow generation power to understand whether all of this will really stick or whether it is just a short-term party.

If we look at the historical distribution dynamics, then the chart below is not that encouraging, to say the least:

PAA has made several cuts before. Meanwhile, peers like EPD and MPLX have managed to preserve their distribution levels across many different cycles.

I think that this is also one of the key drivers for PAA's discount (EV/EBITDA of 8.7x - lower than for most other competitors) and thus above-average yield of about 9.5%.

Yet, let me now elaborate on some recent news and share my thoughts on why I believe that this time things are different with PAA. I know that by saying the phrase "this time it's different" it already sounds suspicious/risky, but bear with me.

Thesis Review

Let's first look at the key figures from Q2, 2025.

Remember, PAA's business is comprised of two parts: crude oil and NGL. The former explains the lion's share of total EBITDA generation, while the latter is more or less a complementary segment.

The core—crude oil—showed yet again strong performance by delivering quarter-to-quarter growth. The quarterly EBITDA ticked up to $580 million from $559 million in Q1. The increase was driven by Permian volume growth and the previously realized bolt-on transactions.

The NGL segment continued to struggle. There is a significant seasonality in play, which accounted for the ~$96 million drop. But even excluding the seasonality component, the adjusted EBITDA decreased by $6 million from the previous quarter, landing now at $87 million.

However, due to the exceptional performance within the core business, PAA has nicely kept the full-year 2025 EBITDA guidance in place - at around $2.9 billion, which is clearly supportive to both current and future (increased) distributions.

Why is this my assumption? Because of the following:

  • The distribution coverage remained very strong at ~175%.
  • Leverage ratio remained in the investment grade territory of 3.3x.
  • Going forward there will be tailwinds for the cash flow growth due to a quite busy period of M&A activity (see explanation below).

In Q2, PAA ventured into a bolt-on acquisition of an additional 20% interest in BridgeTex Pipeline Company LLC - paying $100 million. Since this is already a cash flow producing joint venture with ONEOK (OKE), we should expect a positive impact already in Q3. With this, on a YTD basis, PAA will have already acquired 5 bolt-on M&A moves with a total consideration of around $800 million. And even with this context, PAA's leverage is still robust at 3.3x.

Apart from this, we obviously have to mention PAA's decision to simplify the business by divesting the NGL arm. From this transaction, PAA is set to access about $3 billion of pure cash after covering deal related taxes and expenses, and, importantly, after paying out a special distribution of $0.35 per unit.

While the deal itself was conducted at an EV/EBITDA of 8.5x - value neutral given PAA's multiple - there are numerous qualitative benefits that PAA will get out of this.

First, part of the proceeds will be directed towards retiring preferreds, which will be positive for distribution coverage. Also, given that Series A preferreds yield a fixed rate of 9.37% and Series B yield SOFR + 4.37%, there should not be any material drag on the current DCF generation.

Second, PAA's cash flow conversion rate is set to improve from 70% to 80% (due to reduced CapEx needs and optimized taxation).

Third, the commodity exposure in PAA's EBITDA will go down from 20% to 15%.

Finally, PAA will now have access to a sizable war chest that could be put to work in specific segments that go hand in hand with its current core business and the overall strategy of keeping the commodity exposure as low as possible.

Risks

Here I would like to remind you what I laid out in the article from the very start. PAA cut its distribution several times ago, which is not a good sign for the sustainability of the current income streams. Even though the previous cuts were largely driven by excessive debt and unfortunate timing from the commodity market perspective (debt part is largely solved due to the IG balance sheet), we still have to appreciate PAA’s exposure to crude oil. I think that you will agree with me that the crude oil segment has become quite vulnerable now due to recessionary risks and, at the same time, strong production volumes. The prices are nowhere near where they were 2 to 3 years ago, which motivated upstream players to venture into sizable new drilling (CAPEX) projects. Now, the prices have come down, but the consequences (fresh supply) have obviously remained. So, if we suddenly experience a real economic slowdown or, say, a peace deal between Ukraine and Russia, the chances for oil dropping significantly lower would increase dramatically (and thus pressure on crude oil players). Yet, the good news is that if we look deeper into PAA’s cash generation profile, we will notice that there are extremely favorable price and volumetric production mechanisms embedded in the Partnership. About 80%+ of EBITDA is linked to take-or-pay contracts, which come with minimum base cash flows that not only are sufficient to cover debt service, but also generate accretive IRR even if commodity markets turn really south.

In essence, I see PAA’s profile de-risked on the financial and business risk front. Granted, in case of a steep correction in the crude oil segment, PAA will suffer, but not to an extent where we would be talking about distribution cut.

The Bottom Line

PAA's key fundamentals indicate that the business is in great shape and durable enough to withstand potential shocks. We are talking here about an investment-grade balance sheet, huge surplus in the distribution coverage, and improved business (on many fronts) after NGL divestiture. In previous instances when PAA was forced to cut distributions, the balance sheet was nowhere near the current level, and the exposure to commodity risk was significantly higher.

Now, what I really like about PAA is that it offers one of the sector-leading yield levels of around 9.5%, which on a forward basis (after factoring in the forthcoming special distribution) is 11.8%. So, by buying PAA, we can lock in a forward double-digit yield, where it is very likely that the actual yield will be even higher due to the potential distribution hike decisions. This is my base assumption considering management's commitment to getting the distribution coverage to 160%, which currently is around 175%.

And, of course, all of this is underpinned by resilient fundamentals.

In my humble opinion, PAA is a clear buy for income-focused investors.

data.title: Legacy Education: The Opportunity To Buy It Cheaply
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Note: This is an update to my previous article where I covered the LGCY business model in more detail.

Legacy Education (NYSE:LGCY) has medical schools and benefits from the shortage of healthcare professionals in the United States and has benefited from the fragmentation of this sector through M&A in addition to growing organically by adding new study programs.

In my previous coverage, the company reported strong results for the third quarter of 2025, triggering a nearly 50% rally, only to fall 30% in recent days due to lower-than-expected profitability in the most recent quarter. That said, there are strong indications that this could be temporary, and I believe the stock has great value at its current price, so it's worth clarifying why the market's negative reaction occurred and why it's a clear opportunity to buy at a low price.

Disappointing Profitability

On September 25, Legacy reported its fourth-quarter results, closing out fiscal year 2025. This quarter saw solid sales growth and a degree of operational stability, but with slight pressure on earnings per share, which the market didn't like at all, the shares fell almost 20% in a single day.

During the quarter, revenues totaled $18.0 million, beating consensus estimates by 7%. EBIT was $1.99 million, in line with expectations, but earnings per share were $0.09, below the $0.11 expected. With these results, the company achieved revenues of $64 million for the year, a 39.5% increase year over year, with net income of $7.5 million, representing a net margin of around 12%. Despite disappointing profitability during the fourth quarter, diluted EPS was $0.59, slightly higher than the $0.53 of the previous year.

Q4 2025 Estimate Actual Beat/miss
Revenue $16.5M $18.0M +6.9%
EBIT $1.98M $1.99M +0.5%
EPS $0.11 $0.09 -18.2%

Regarding profitability, it's worth noting that Legacy closed the year close to the highest gross margin since 2019, but operating expenses were double the previous year ($6.3 million vs. $3.3 million). Management comments that an increase of $700,000 was mainly due to a one-time increase in reserves for student accounts receivable. This happened because management noticed signs that graduates were paying more slowly than usual and decided to create a provision for bad debts. However, this doesn't mean the company lost that money, and if students pay later, that amount could be reversed.

If we add this amount to net income to slightly normalize the valuation, the company would have earned $1.93 million in the fourth quarter instead of $1.23 million. This equates to $8.24 million in net income for the full year 2025, which, when divided by the current market capitalization of $116 million, would result in a P/E ratio of 20 times 2025 earnings. Not bad for a company that grew sales 40% during that year.

Growth Prospects Are Still Positive

Beyond these accounting adjustments, Legacy closed the year with positive growth prospects. The company reached a record enrollment of 3,101 students, a 42% increase over the previous year, driven by demand for health-related programs. This performance confirms the successful integration of Contra Costa College, which added 468 students and 14 new programs, and it's positive considering possible future acquisitions and the integration that these would entail.

The company also received initial accreditation from the National League for Nursing Commission on Nursing Education Accreditation (NLN CNEA) for its Registered Nursing program, an achievement that expands its nursing education portfolio and demonstrates the company's credibility and reputation with regulatory bodies. At the same time, the company obtained approval for three new degree programs and two certificate programs, scheduled to launch in October 2025, which will be important for attracting new students through increased coursework options.

Valuation

Now that we know there's nothing wrong with the company and the overall results look good, I think it's reasonable to estimate that the company will grow at least 20% over the next few years and maintain net margins of 12% (during Q1 and Q3 2025 these reached 15%). This means that in five years, it would be earning close to $20 million in profit, divided by the current 12.7 million shares outstanding, results in EPS of $1.50. Apply a P/E of 15 times, and the shares could be worth more than double the current price. Now imagine what it could be worth if the company grows at recent rates or improves its net margins.

Risks

The most immediate risk is the company's cost structure. General and administrative expenses often increase due to marketing expenditures to attract new students in a highly competitive market with relatively little differentiation and professional fees to retain quality teachers. While some of these costs are nonrecurring and may be subject to cost controls, sustained expense growth could put pressure on margins if revenue growth normalizes.

Legacy still operates primarily in six campuses across California, exposing it to regional economic problems and potential regulatory changes at the state level. Geographic concentration remains a key strategic limitation until the company executes new acquisitions outside the state and proves that it has the necessary know-how.

Also, I'd like to point out that share dilution has been significant, with the diluted share count up 31% year over year during the full year 2025. If the company continues to use stock for acquisitions or executive compensation, EPS growth may lag behind operating income, reducing shareholder returns even if there's solid revenue growth.

The Bottom Line

Although profitability was reduced during Q4 2025, and the reason may suggest that students are struggling to pay their tuition, I think it's too early to render a verdict, and the rest of the operating performance still looks healthy and solid. If we take the current valuation into account, it appears that the recent price drop provided another buying opportunity after the stock doubled between April and September and had stopped looking so cheap.

data.title: Tesla, Netflix Scheduled To Report Earnings Next Week As Investors Focus On Delayed CPI
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

U.S. stocks opened mixed on Friday, as investor anxiety mounted over the financial health of regional banks, fueled by rising credit risk concerns. Thursday's session ended on a sour note, with major indexes closing lower amid heightened volatility. Investors reacted to negative developments, including ongoing tensions in the U.S.-China trade dispute, troubling loan data from several banks, and continued analysis of corporate earnings. The prolonged U.S. government shutdown continues to weigh on market confidence as it stretches into a third week, adding further uncertainty for investors.

Amid a U.S. government shutdown, investors will keep a close eye on economic reports that will be released next week; however, government numbers are in doubt as the shutdown continues. The economic reports that are expected to be announced next week include Business Inventories, Industrial Production, and Retail Inventories on Monday, with the Redbook Index on Tuesday and MBA Mortgage Applications on Wednesday. Thursday will see the release of Chicago Fed National Activity and Existing Home Sales, while Friday will see the release of Core CPI, Real Earnings, S&P Global Manufacturing PMI, and Michigan Consumer Sentiment.

Netflix (NFLX), Coca-Cola (KO), Philip Morris (PM), 3M (MMM), Lockheed Martin (LMT), General Motors (GM), Tesla (TSLA), IBM (IBM), AT&T (T), T-Mobile US (TMUS), Blackstone (BX), Intel (INTC), and Honeywell (HON) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, Oct 20: Steel Dynamics (STLD), Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF), Preferred Bank (PFBC). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, Oct 21: Netflix, Coca-Cola, Philip Morris, 3M, Lockheed Martin, and General Motors. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, Oct 22: Tesla, IBM, and AT&T. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, Oct 23: T-Mobile US, Blackstone, Intel, Honeywell. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, Oct 24: Procter & Gamble (PG), General Dynamics (GD). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Reddit (RDDT) and Biohaven (BHVN) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Investview (OTCQB:INVU), Praxis Precision (PRAX), and Vine Hill Capital Investment (VCIC). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Jin Medical (ZJYL), Graphic Packaging (GPK), and Tvardi Therapeutics (TVRD). Hertz Global (HTZ) and SoundHound AI (SOUN) head into the week with a high level of short interest outstanding.

IPO watch: The IPO is slowing down due to the impact of the government shutdown, with no new debuts expected in the week ahead. The analyst quiet period expires on Knorex (KNRX) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Caterpillar (CAT), Dell Technologies (DELL), CVS Health (CVS), and Procter & Gamble (PG).

Netflix Earnings Preview: Streaming giant Netflix is set to report its third-quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting the company to report earnings per share of $6.94 on revenue of $11.51B. Seaport recently upgraded Netflix stock to "buy" from a previous investment rating of "neutral" and increased the ad revenue estimates for NFLX to more closely track the TV viewership share. Analysts also noted that engagement on the streaming giant's platform continues to be strong, with at least a couple of 'cultural zeitgeist' titles driving viewership share and possibly subscriber net addition gains.

Tesla Earnings Preview: EV maker Tesla is scheduled to report its third-quarter earnings next week and is expected to report earnings per share of $0.55 on revenue of $26.58B. Seeking Alpha analyst Victor Dergunov believes that Tesla has enormous growth potential in industries like AI, EVs, FSD, robotics, energy generation, and storage. Meanwhile, SA analyst Louis Gerard pointed out in a recent analysis that Tesla's profitability and capital efficiency have sharply declined, with margins and returns now resembling traditional automakers rather than high-growth tech firms.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Power The American Dream And Collect 8% Yields
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Small businesses represent 43.5% of the American GDP and are responsible for employing 46% of the nation’s workforce (Source: US Chamber of Commerce). Given the magnitude of their overall influence, small businesses indeed constitute the backbone of the economy.

Middle-market companies are most vulnerable to supply chain disruptions since they lack the financial capacity to make quick changes. Moreover, they often have fewer customers with significant revenue concentration, causing a negative impact if customers were to make profound changes to their supply chains.

Banks typically don’t lend to middle-market companies, and these enterprises don’t have the luxury of tapping into the equities or the bond markets either. This constrains their ability to raise funds to grow, expand, and flourish. During the 1980s, the U.S. Congress amended the Investment Company Act of 1940 – it paved the way for business development companies. These institutions were created to provide investor capital to middle-market companies and are required by law to have at least 70% of their assets invested in non-public U.S. firms with market values under $250 million. And as long as they distribute 90% of their income to investors, they're not taxed at the corporate level.

Pick #1: BIZD – Yield 12.3%

As an industry, BDCs typically borrow at fixed rates and lend at floating rates. While the overall exposure to fixed-rate loans and floating-rate debt varies from BDC to BDC, historically we have seen middle-market companies borrow more when rates are lower. BDCs ended Q2 2025 with their average leverage at the lower end of their historic average, keeping room for expansion as borrowing activity increases. You see, a BDC can make more money from a few borrowers when rates are higher, but they can also make more money by lending to more borrowers when rates are lower. According to a recent report from the Bank of America Institute, small businesses are spending more on technology services, particularly AI, with marketing and advertising being the top use cases. We expect rate cuts to fuel greater origination activity given that middle-market companies will look to participate in technology adoption and to benefit from more manufacturing and industrial projects coming to America. To build data centers, manufacturing facilities, warehouses, and other establishments, massive construction projects need to be undertaken, and to make them operational requires significant domestic supply chains. We expect middle markets to effectively participate in these activities.

Since BDCs provide investors access to private credit, they're considered a type of closed-end fund. VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) is a fund-of-funds that invests in 30 public BDCs, with its top 10 holdings representing ~74% of its invested assets. Source

BIZD’s allocations are market-cap weighted, meaning the largest public BDCs will be its biggest allocations. This ETF gives you passive, unleveraged access to the BDC sector, allowing you to collect the payments from its holdings minus a small management fee. Since they distribute from what they collect, the ETF maintains a variable distribution policy.

Yes, contrary to what most investors perceive, BIZD's expenses stand at a modest 0.42%. While total operating expenses may show as 12.86%, alarming most readers and immediately making them dismiss the investment altogether, the bulk of this represents acquired fund fees and expenses. The ETF maintains a dedicated FAQ section to address this concern, outlining that they are required by SEC rules to report a total expense ratio that accounts for the expense ratios of all the underlying holdings. This means BIZD’s expense ratio will include the expenses incurred by the likes of Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and others, despite not paying those expenses out of their pocket.

Since its IPO in 2013, the BDC has maintained an average of 100% of its distributions sourced from dividend income with minuscule use of return of capital likely to adjust for dividend collection vs. distribution dates.

Since 2016, BIZD has maintained relatively steady distribution and price levels, averaging a 9.9% yield. Source

In recent months, the slowing U.S. economy (signaling recession possibilities), fears of growing credit risks, and Fed rate cuts have resulted in a BDC sector sell-off. At current price levels, this ETF sports a 12%-plus yield, presenting an opportunity to lock in higher returns as American middle market companies require more capital to adapt to evolving economic circumstances.

Again, BDCs are inherently a type of closed-end fund. Hence, they're required by regulation to have a coverage ratio of at least 200% on their preferred securities. Failing to do so will force them to stop paying dividends to common shareholders (a critical requirement for a functional BDC) until regaining the coverage. Also, when it comes to taking on debt, the leverage ratio cannot exceed 33% of total assets (implying a 300% asset coverage). This provides a high degree of safety for investors higher up in their capital structure, making BDC preferreds and baby bonds among the safest fixed-income securities available. We will now discuss two solid yields in this category.

Pick #2: RWAY Baby Bonds – Up To 7.9% Yields

Runway Growth Finance Corp. (RWAY) is a smaller BDC focused on providing financing to late-stage and growth companies. The company ended the second quarter with an aggregate fair value of $1 billion, with its assets invested in a portfolio of mostly debt made to 54 companies. According to the earnings report, 97.8% of the BDC’s investments were in senior secured loans, providing asset backup for the loans. It's also notable that RWAY typically makes loans to borrowers with loan-to-value below 30%, with the weighted average borrower LTV at 21.1% as of Q2 2025. This provides good asset cover for its debt assets.

The BDC reported $297.0 million in available liquidity at the end of Q2, and spent $22 million on interest expenses during the first half of 2025. RWAY has two public baby bonds, rated BBB+ by Egan-Jones.

  • Runway Growth Finance Corp. 7.50% Notes Due 7/28/2027 (RWAYL) – Yield 7.4%

  • Runway Growth Finance Corp. 8% Notes due 2027 (RWAYZ) – Yield 7.9%

Pick #3: TRIN Baby Bonds – Up To 7.8% Yields

Trinity Capital (TRIN) has over $2.3 billion in AUM and a history of steady dividend increases since its IPO in 2020. The BDC focuses on early growth companies with some kind of institutional backing. Its borrowers typically have modest profitability and seek capital to scale their operations.

The BDC ended Q2 with $143.3 million in available liquidity, and spent $35.7 million in interest expenses during 1H 2025. For this period, TRIN reported $67.1 million in net investment income after factoring in debt expenses, implying solid coverage for its bond interest payments.

TRIN has two public baby bonds, rated BBB by Morningstar DBRS. Both trade at a slight premium to par but aren’t callable until next year, with maturities set in 2029.

  • 7.875% Notes Due 2029 (TRINZ) – Yield 7.8%

  • 7.875% Notes Due 2029 (TRINI) – Yield 7.8%

Conclusion

The United States is the largest economy in the world, and its economic engine runs on the backs of middle-market companies. Yet, their access to credit and capital depends on specialized investment vehicles like BDCs. As the Fed continues rate cuts, we expect greater demand for capital, and BDCs will see increasing loan origination volume.

The American Dream is built on the belief that anyone, from anywhere, can turn ambition into achievement through hard work. Middle-market companies are living examples of the dream built by entrepreneurs. By investing in BDCs, I'm helping them support the American Dream while generating a reliable stream of income to support the retirement of my dreams.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we build our passive income by investing across the entire capital structure of a wide range of companies and funds. While this article discusses a few of our BDC holdings, our model portfolio comprises over 45 income-producing securities, with an overall yield of over 9%. Diversification is how you take control of your income and your retirement on your terms, not Wall Street’s. This is the power of income investing.

data.title: IEUR: A Cheap Way To Add European Exposure Ahead Of Trump-Putin Talks
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, the iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF (NYSEARCA:IEUR) has significantly outperformed the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), delivering a gain of roughly 30% in USD terms, roughly double the return of the S&P 500-tracking ETF:

This comes even as the main risk facing Europe, the ongoing war in Ukraine, is arguably no closer to being resolved, despite numerous attempts of a negotiated settlement since 2022. That said, an upcoming meeting between Trump and Putin in Hungary provides another chance of reaching a ceasefire, which would be very bullish for IEUR. I rank IEUR a Buy, driven by:

  • IEUR holdings trading at only 17.95x their trailing earnings, a meaningful discount to the S&P 500.
  • Favorable GDP growth outlook for 2026, which should benefit the ETF's cyclical holdings.
  • A well-covered 2.89% current dividend yield, with dividend growth likely to marginally accelerate relative to the past ten years.

In this article, I will expand on these points and discuss risks related to the investment case.

ETF Overview

You can access all IEUR details on the iShares website here. The iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF invests in small-, mid-, and large-cap European stocks (its benchmark is the MSCI Europe Investable Market Index). From the start I should note that IEUR represents an implicit short USD bet, since the ETF is traded in USD, but its value depends on European stocks, which are traded in local currencies such as the British pound, euro, and Swiss franc.

From a geographic perspective, the ETF's largest exposure is to the United Kingdom (22.76% of ETF assets), France (15.73%), Germany (14.20%), and Switzerland (14.01%):

We observe that the geographic allocation is exclusively to developed markets in Europe, which come with high institutional stability. On the flip side, you are missing out on emerging or even frontier markets in the rest of the continent, which typically trade at lower valuations but also come with governance risks.

From a sector perspective, IEUR exhibits a pronounced cyclical tilt, as the largest sector is financials (22.21% of ETF assets), followed by industrials at 19.87%. This is somewhat offset by a sizable allocation to health care (13.05%) and consumer staples (8.95%):

The overweight position in cyclical sectors makes IEUR a solid pick when economic growth is robust. At the same time, if the European economy loses economic momentum, IEUR is likely to underperform other ETFs with more defensive positioning.

The ETF currently has 1,010 individual positions, which, for an ETF with an expense ratio of 0.09%, is quite impressive. As such, IEUR offers good diversification (top ten positions account for only 17.53% of assets) at a very low price. When discussing ETFs with friends, I always recommend ETFs with expenses of 0.1-0.2% for long-term passive holdings. In this respect, IEUR clearly ticks all the boxes.

Current Valuation

IEUR holdings currently trade at a trailing P/E of 17.95x, implying a trailing earnings yield of roughly 5.57%. Relative to the S&P 500, European stocks remain much cheaper, as the leading U.S. benchmark trades at roughly 28.4x its trailing earnings, or a trailing earnings yield of circa 3.52%.

As such, S&P 500 earnings would have to grow some 2% higher in perpetuity just to make up for higher starting valuations, something I see as unsustainable over the long term. That said, IEUR only has a 7.45% allocation to the information technology sector, 27.64% lower than the S&P 500. As such, should AI & automation growth pan out, odds are that IEUR earnings growth will indeed be much slower than that of the leading U.S. benchmark.

From a dividend perspective, IEUR offers a well-covered 2.89% current yield, which has grown at 5.31% over the past decade, only 0.49% weaker than the 5.80% growth rate achieved by the SPY:

I estimate that IEUR's payout ratio stands at 0.54x, indicating that the current dividend yield is well-covered. Factoring in retained earnings, 2% inflation and GDP growth marginally above 1% for developed European countries, it is feasible to believe that dividends continue to grow at about 5.6% over the next decade, a circa 0.3% increase relative to dividend growth in the preceding ten years.

2026 Growth Outlook

In the near term, the IMF expects the UK and Switzerland to deliver GDP growth of 1.3% in 2026, marginally ahead of Germany and France, each seen growing at 0.9%:

As such, also factoring in growth expectations for the remainder of IEUR's country allocations, we observe that countries in which IEUR invests should grow by roughly 1.1-1.2% in 2026, circa 0.85% below the growth rate in the United States, which is forecast to see GDP growth of 2% next year.

While country GDP growth is an imperfect proxy for earnings growth, I believe that the marginally weaker growth rate in Europe in 2026 is not enough to justify the significant valuation discount IEUR holdings trade at.

IEUR Rating

Despite meaningful year-to-date outperformance, IEUR holdings remain cheap relative to the S&P 500, offering an earnings yield roughly 2% higher than the leading U.S. benchmark.

GDP growth expectations for 2026 (which are largely in line with what you would expect in a typical year) point to European GDP growth underperforming the U.S. by less than one percent, not enough to justify the current valuation discount.

As such, as long as you are comfortable with the underweight position in information technology, respectively the overweight position in cyclical sectors such as financials/industrials, I believe that a Buy rating is warranted for IEUR.

Over the near term, IEUR may also benefit from a favorable outcome from the upcoming peace negotiations in Hungary.

Risks

The principal risk to consider when investing in IEUR is that GDP growth may fall short of expectations. In such an environment, the ETF's cyclical holdings will do poorly, resulting in weak returns for investors.

While a weak USD (as evidenced by the circa 9% slump in the U.S. dollar index (DXY)) has been a tailwind for IEUR returns this year, a reversal of recent trends may result in U.S. investors facing losses. As such, IEUR is not an ETF suitable for investors who do not want to bear currency risk:

Last but not least, geopolitical risk, principally stemming from the war in Ukraine, remains a central concern for investors in European equities. With IEUR outperforming the S&P 500 this year, it is clear that some of this risk has been priced out by markets recently, exposing investors to losses if fighting escalates.

Conclusion

European equities are finally outperforming their U.S. peers, benefiting from low starting valuations and USD weakness. While geopolitical risks remain a central concern when investing in Europe, I believe that sooner or later the war in Ukraine will end, providing a tailwind for European stocks.

IEUR holdings remain attractively valued relative to the S&P 500. The ETF offers a well-covered 2.89% current dividend yield, with my estimates pointing to 5.60% dividend growth over the next decade, marginally stronger than the 5.31% increase achieved over the past ten years.

I rank IEUR a Buy for U.S. investors comfortable with currency risk. The ETF's overweight position in cyclical sectors is another risk factor to consider in light of the inherent uncertainty in the economic growth outlook.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Is Lloyds Banking Group Still An Attractive Income Play Ahead Of Q3 2025 Earnings? (Downgrade)
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article about one year ago, Lloyds Banking Group (NYSE:LYG) was an interesting income pick in the European banking sector at the time, due to a good combination of yield and value. Not surprisingly, its shares have performed quite well since then, beating the overall stock market during the same period for a wide margin.

As the bank is expected to report its quarterly earnings over the next few days, I think it’s now a good time to revisit its investment case and update its most recent financial performance, to see if it remains a good income pick within the European banking sector.

Financial Overview

During the first half of 2025 (H1 2025), Lloyds has reported a positive operating momentum supported by higher top-line and good cost control. Indeed, its total revenues increased by 6% YoY to more than £8.9 billion in the semester, due to increasing net interest income (NII) and other revenues.

As I’ve covered recently on other peers including NatWest (NWG) or Barclays (BCS), despite lower rates from the Bank of England over the past few quarters, U.K. banks have been able to report higher NII due to positive lending growth and also supported by actions related to their treasury books, namely the reinvestment of maturing securities at higher yields. These have been important factors for NII resilience during H1 2025 among the sector, with Lloyds being no exception.

Its lending book increased by 3% YoY to £471 billion in H1 2025 while its structural hedge portfolio amounted to £244 billion, up by 0.8% from the end of 2024, leading to higher average interest-earning assets. On the deposits side, Lloyds reported inflows of about £11 billion during H1 2025, to total deposits of £494 billion at the end of last June. Higher customer deposits are also positive for margins because banks usually don’t pass on to customers the same rate they can get from lending or investing in securities, especially related to retail deposits that largely stay in current accounts with very low or no remuneration. This background explains why Lloyds’ gross margin on deposits increased to 1.29% in H1 2025 (vs. 1.13% in the first half of 2024), being positive for NII growth in recent quarters.

Considering this background, it’s not surprising to see that Lloyds’ NII increased by 5% YoY, to £6.65 billion in H1 2025, representing about 75% of the bank’s total revenues in the first half of 2025. This is a high weight compared to other banks that have a more diversified revenue stream, making it more exposed to rates. However, as a retail-focused bank that practically only operates in its domestic market, this is not surprising and is not expected to change much in the future.

Having said that, investors should be aware that a large part of its lending book is set at fixed rates, plus the structural hedge portfolio represents about 27% of the bank’s total assets, making its NII sensitivity to rates lower than could be assumed when just looking at NII’s weight on total revenues. This explains why despite headwinds from falling rates in the U.K., as the Bank of England has cut its key rate several times over the past few months to a current level of 4% (vs. a peak level of 5.25% in August 2023), Lloyds’ NII is expected to grow gradually over the next few quarters. According to analysts’ estimates, the bank’s NII should be around £3.4 billion in Q3 2025 (up by 1.9% over the previous quarter) and grow to close to £3.5 billion in Q4. For the full year, Lloyds’ guidance is to reach NII around £13.5 billion (up by 5.7% YoY), while for 2026 the bank has not yet given any guidance, but probably it will target mid-single digit growth.

Regarding other revenues, the bank has been pushing for cross-selling opportunities across its customer base, pushing for insurance, wealth management, and other products. This strategy has delivered good results, and its positive trend has been maintained over the past few quarters. In H1 2025, other income increased to about £2.97 billion, up by 9% YoY, supported by retail customers and higher fees in insurance, pensions, and investments. Despite this positive momentum, the market is expecting some slowdown in the coming quarters to a low mid-single-digit growth rate, showing that growth opportunities outside core banking products may be harder to achieve.

Indeed, while Lloyds has a strong position in the banking sector, in other segments it has more difficulty to gain market share and grow its business. For instance, Lloyds has a partnership with Schroders (OTCPK:SHNWF) in the wealth management segment that was launched back in 2019 but has recently reached an agreement to take full ownership of the company, which will be renamed Lloyds Wealth. This happened as growth targets were not met and there was significant management turnaround, and the bank now believes it can achieve better results on its own rather than partnering with Schroders. While this was not a positive experience for the bank, as assets under management barely grew over the past few years, it gives more flexibility for Lloyds to cross-sell products to affluent customers in the future and, potentially, achieve higher growth than was possible before.

Regarding costs and credit quality, the bank has reported a positive development in recent quarters. In H1 2025, its operating expenses declined by 2% YoY to £4.9 billion, leading to a cost-to-income ratio of 55% which is acceptable compared to other European banks, but it’s higher than compared to its closest peer NatWest (which reported a cost-to-income ratio of 50% in the first half of 2025). This means the bank should continue to invest in digitalization to improve its operating efficiency, targeting an efficiency ratio below 50% by 2026. However, as revenue growth is not expected to be strong over the coming quarters and cost reductions aren’t easy in the current inflationary environment, its efficiency ratio is expected to improve to 51% in 2026 but to miss its own target, showing that further work on cost reductions may be needed.

On the credit quality front, Lloyds has reported relatively low levels of credit losses as the U.K economy has been resilient, reporting impairment charges of £442 million in H1 2025, or about 19 basis points (bps) of average loans. Its guidance for the full year is for impairment charges to be around 25 bps, which means the bank expects some credit quality deterioration in the second half. As the bank has some significant exposure to credit cards and commercial banking, this is not an unexpected trend, but credit losses seem to be at manageable levels, even though credit impairments are expected to increase during the second half of the year.

Its net income increased by 4% YoY to €2.54 billion in H1 2025, not an impressive growth rate, and is expected to decline on a quarterly basis due to higher credit impairments. I think this is a factor that is important to monitor ahead, being quite likely that the bank’s management will update its expectations regarding asset quality for the rest of the year and 2026 during its Q3 2025 earnings call.

Current street estimates are for the bank to report credit impairments of about £1.1 billion in 2025 and grow to about £1.3 billion next year, but the bank has not yet given guidance related to asset quality for 2026. Thus, these estimates are likely to be revised upwards as there has been some deterioration in credit quality recently, potentially being a headwind for earnings growth in 2026.

As shown in the previous figure, the bank has a good capital generation and this is something that is not expected to change going forward, expecting to generate about 175 bps of capital this year and more than 200 bps in 2026. Its CET1 ratio was 13.8% at the end of last June (vs. 13.5% at the end of 2024), being well within its target between 13.5%-14%.

Therefore, Lloyds does not need to retain many earnings and can distribute a large part of annual earnings to shareholders, both through dividends and share buybacks. This has been its strategy in recent years, returning between £3.4-3.8 billion per year over the last four years, a strategy that is not expected to change much in the near term.

Its recent interim dividend related to 2025 earnings was raised by 15% YoY to £1.22 per share, being a good sign that Lloyds wants to maintain a growing dividend. Related to 2025 earnings, the street expects the bank to deliver a total dividend of about £3.6 per share, leading to a forward dividend yield of about 4.3%. While this is still an interesting yield for income investors, it’s lower than when I last analyzed it (it was yielding around 5.2% at the time) and is also slightly lower than compared to NatWest, which offers a forward dividend yield around 4.5%, showing that Lloyds’ income appeal is not fantastic.

In my opinion, this is more a reflection of a strong share price rally over the past year rather than a shift in the bank’s capital return policy, being a signal that its shares may no longer be undervalued.

Indeed, Lloyds is currently trading at about 1.2 book value, while it was trading below book value when I last analyzed it. This shows that its strong share price rally was not supported by a rising book value and is trading now at a valuation that seems to be fair given the bank’s profitability level. This is also a very similar valuation to NatWest, which is its closest peer, being another signal that Lloyds’ seems to be fairly valued right now.

Conclusion

Lloyds Banking Group has a relatively low-risk profile within the European banking sector and also low growth prospects, making income the most attractive feature of its investment case. However, due to a strong share price rally in recent months, its valuation and dividend yield aren’t that attractive to long-term income investors. Thus, considering the declining interest rate environment ahead in the U.K. and the risk of higher provisions for loan losses impacting its earnings growth over the coming quarters, I downgrade my recommendation on Lloyds to ‘Hold’ as upside potential seems limited for the time being.

data.title: Abbott: GLP-1 Beneficiary - A Dividend Raise May Be Coming
data.description:
I previously covered Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) in April 2025, discussing how it continued to offer a compelling high-growth investment thesis, driven by the GLP-1 tailwinds on the Continuous Glucose Monitoring and the adult nutrition offerings.

Combined with the reiterated ambitious FY2025 guidance, the richer Free Cash Flow generation, the healthier balance sheet, and the high single-digit dividend per share growth, I had believed that the stock remained well positioned to outperform ahead, resulting in my buy rating then.

In this article, I shall be discussing how the market has overreacted to ABT's lowered FY2025 organic sales growth/narrowed adj. EPS, since the management continues to deliver robust numbers while building upon FY2024 outperformance.

With new launches generating "nearly $0.5 billion in sales this quarter and adding more than 100 basis points to organic sales growth" and the management's comfort with the "consensus sales and EPS (estimates) for 2026," thanks to the easier YoY comparison and new innovations, I believe that its breakout may soon occur with the sideways trading since early 2025 nearly ending, resulting in my reiterated Buy rating here.

ABT Delivers Renewed Growth Through New Launches, As It Also Capitalizes On GLP-1 Trends

ABT 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, ABT has mostly traded sideways at -1.16% against that of the wider market at +27.4%, attributed to the steep -8.5% pullback after the poorly received FQ2'25 earnings call in July 2025 and once more, after the recent FQ3'25 earnings call.

Much of its headwinds are attributed to the lowered FY2025 organic sales growth guidance to +7.75% YoY at the midpoint and the narrowed adj EPS range (yet stable on a midpoint basis at $5.15) at between $5.12 and $5.18 (+10.2% YoY at the midpoint).

When comparing to the original guidance of +8% at the midpoint and range of $5.05 to $5.25 offered in the FQ4'24 earnings call, it is apparent that the pessimism embedded in ABT's stock prices has been overdone, since the management continues to deliver robust top/bottom-line growth while building upon the FY2024 top/bottom-line growth profile at +9.6% YoY and +5.1% YoY, respectively.

If anything, I am of the opinion that the accelerating adj. EPS growth against the 5Y/10Y historical means has been extremely impressive, despite the -0.4 point tariff impact on its gross margins to 55.8% in FQ3'25.

This is especially since ABT has been able to balance the $200M tariff headwinds through its highly efficient operational efforts, as observed in the notably richer adj. operating margin at 23% in FQ3'25 (+0.1 points QoQ/+0.4 YoY).

Much of its tailwinds are attributed to the robust demand for its diabetes care offerings across:

  • Continuous glucose monitors at +17.2% YoY sales growth (despite the tougher YoY comparison from a year ago at +20.7% YoY) and
  • Adult Nutrition by +5.4% YoY sales growth (compared to a year ago at +9.1% YoY), through Ensure (complete and balanced nutrition) and Glucerna (as part of a balanced diet and blood sugar management plan).

Particularly, consumer demand for Adult Nutrition has been accelerating in international markets, as observed in the +10% YoY growth in FQ3'25, which perhaps explains why ABT has sought to capitalize on the growing demand through new Ensure launches with 42 grams of protein and Glucerna launches with only 1 gram of sugar.

These numbers exemplify the company's ability to capitalize on the ongoing GLP-1 care boom, thanks to the complementary GLP-1 care across diagnostics and nutrition.

Beyond the GLP-1 related offerings, ABT has also been driving renewed growth with new launches generating "nearly $0.5 billion in sales this quarter and adding more than 100 basis points to organic sales growth," with it naturally lending credence to the company's growing R&D efforts by +7.5% YoY in FQ3'25.

The same has been observed in its new product launches across the other segments, including:

  • Double digit growth in the Volt PFA catheter for Cardiac Rhythm Management in the EU, with FY2026 likely to benefit from the US launch,
  • market share gains for TAVR valve Navitor in Structural Heart, aided by the recently expanded indication to treat people who are at low/intermediate risk for open-heart surgery along with two ongoing clinical trials,
  • Lingo as an over-the-counter biowearables for those without diabetes, allowing users to track their glucose readings (with future plans to expand to additional biomarkers, such as ketones, lactate, and alcohol), and
  • biosimilars in Established Pharmaceuticals, which recently received regulatory approvals and are set for launches across different countries in H2'25, amongst others.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why ABT's management has highlighted their comfort with the "consensus sales and EPS (estimates) for 2026," with it implying another robust growth profile at +7.6% YoY and +9.9% YoY ahead.

Combined with the easier YoY comparison from FQ4'25 onwards and the picking up of China sales, it is unsurprising that the consensus forward estimates appear to be rather promising, with the healthcare company expected to report robust top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +7.2%/+10.3% through FY2027.

This is especially since the management continues to hint at overall CGM sales growth plans from FQ3'25 annualized levels of $8B to 2028 target of $10B, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +7.7%.

ABT Valuations

Given ABT's well diversified capabilities across the nutrition/biosimilar pharmaceutical/medical monitoring device/diagnostics end markets, I can understand why the stock continues to trade at a slight premium over the sector P/E medians at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 25.91x.

Even so, readers may want to note that the stock continues to trade close to the 5Y historical mean of 24.52x and the 10Y mean of 23.10x, with it offering interested investors an excellent margin of safety.

Most importantly, given the top/bottom-line acceleration from the 5Y mean of +5.6%/+7.6% and 10Y means of +7.6%/+9%, respectively, I believe that ABT remains reasonably valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.51x as well (based on the consensus adj EPS growth profile at 3Y CAGR of +10.3%), compared to the 5Y mean of 2.84x and the 10Y mean of 2.59x.

These reasons are also why I believe that ABT's recent sideways trading has been a boon for those looking to add.

ABT Remains Attractively Valued Thanks To The Sideways Trading

ABT 5Y Stock Price

For now, ABT has been consistently well supported in its upward momentum from the late 2023 bottom, with the recent sideways trading since the early 2025 surge being well warranted as it allows the stock to grow into its priced-in premium over the past few quarters.

ABT's Technical Indicators

The same has been observed in its technical indicators and historical trends as well, with ABT likely to break out after the next bounce off the uptrend support lines of $120s.

Most importantly, if historical trends persist, the company is likely to announce a dividend hike soon, with investors potentially looking forward to another rich raise of +7.2%, as per the last increase, if not the 10-year historical growth of +9.4%.

ABT's dividend investment thesis remains secure as well, with the annualized dividend obligation of $4.12B well covered by the annualized H1'25 free cash flow generation of $4.96B (+20.2% YoY), significantly aided by the healthier balance sheet at the last reported net debt position of -$6.15B in FQ2'25 (+18.3% YoY), with detailed FQ3'25 10Q yet available.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 25.91x and the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $6.26, there remains an excellent upside potential of +25.7% to my long-term price target of $162.10 as well, with it exemplifying its robust shareholder return prospects across capital appreciation and dividend income.

As a result, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the ABT stock here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: Booking: Growing Market Share And Traveler Wallet Share In Alternative Accommodations And Through Its Connected Trip Vision
data.description:
Overview

Much has been written about Airbnb Inc.'s (ABNB) role in bringing alternative accommodation into the mainstream and its continued expansion into experiences and services. Meanwhile, Booking Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:BKNG) has kept its head down, continuing to focus on enhancing its core businesses of accommodation, flights, car rentals, and restaurant reservations, while outsourcing travel activities to Viator and local aggregators.

Yet over the last 2 years, Booking’s share price has appreciated by over 80%, while Airbnb was down by 10%.

I revisited my investment thesis for Booking, and concluded that it remains intact.

  • Booking is expanding its leadership in the online travel agency segment [OTA], widening its advantage over smaller competitors due to powerful network effects and operating leverage; and
  • Booking continues to grow briskly by taking market share in the alternative accommodation space from Airbnb.

Furthermore, Booking is insulated against recent developments and well-positioned for the future:

  • Booking is hedged against cities cracking down on short term alternative accommodations and rentals because of its strong presence in traditional hotel accommodations;
  • Booking’s strong presence outside the US insulates it from the near-term drop in international visitors to the United States; and
  • Booking’s Connected Trip initiative will create more cross-selling opportunities in the short term, capturing more wallet share and creating a seamless traveling planning experience for travelers over the long term.

Booking is a long-term holding in my portfolio, as I believe it will continue compounding for the foreseeable future. I intend to take advantage of pullbacks to increase my exposure.

I will elaborate on each of these key points.

Updates

Booking is the undisputed market leader

Booking is substantially larger than its competitors and has almost twice the revenue of Expedia (EXPE), its closest competitor (orange vs purple line).

Booking also reports more than twice the room nights than Airbnb, and thrice the room nights compared to Expedia:

Room nights booked (in millions)

Company

3Q2024

4Q2024

1Q2025

2Q2025

BKNG

299

261

319

309

ABNB (1)

122.8

111.0

143.1

134.4

EXPE

97.4

86.4

107.7

105.5

Note (1): Includes services and experiences

I and many others have written extensively about the powerful network effects in the OTA space (more properties attract more customers, which in turn draw more property owners to list on the site, which then draws more customers, resulting in a virtuous cycle with wide competitive moats), as well as the operating leverage (the incremental cost to serve one additional customer is near-zero). As such, I see no reason to belabor either point other than to point out that these advantages have enabled Booking to generate substantially higher and expanding margins compared to its smaller competitors (orange line).

Booking continues to grow in the alternative accommodation space

Booking has continued to gain share in the alternative accommodations—the segment popularized by Airbnb. According to data provided by both companies, Booking’s share of alternative accommodation room nights (as a percentage of the combined sum of Booking and Airbnb’s room nights) has steadily increased to about 46% for the quarter ended June 2025 (highlighted yellow column).

Furthermore, Booking has been capturing more than half of the incremental room nights (as a percentage of the combined sum of Booking and Airbnb incremental room nights) (highlighted red column).

Two observations:

Airbnb room nights include both (1) its experiences and services, and (2) long-term stays, which is not an area of focus for Booking. As such, Booking’s share of the short-term alternative accommodation space is likely under-estimated.

Booking offers a choice of both traditional and alternative accommodations in mainland China—a fast growing market that Airbnb exited in June 2022. While the decision made sense for Airbnb at that time, it will be exceedingly difficult for the company to regain the trust and confidence of Chinese hosts who were left abandoned while the country was still recovering from the COVID-19 pandemic.

As search for Airbnb homes in Beijing returns the following:

Hedged against ban of short-term rentals

Many countries and cities around the world are suffering from over-tourism and a housing shortage, which governments place blame on Airbnb operators for taking housing supply away from local residents. Some cities, including Amsterdam, Paris, Berlin, Barcelona, Lisbon, Singapore, have implemented restrictions on short term alternative accommodations, ranging from tight regulations (limited number of days, host presence required) to outright bans. Unlike Airbnb, Booking can continue to offer customers traditional hotel accommodations in these markets.

Less reliance on the US market

According to a US Congressional Report entitled Recent Developments in International Travel to the United States, the Trump Administration's continued focus on immigration enforcement has led to concerns about a dampening effect on international tourist visits to the United States. The number of international visitors to the United States has dipped since February 2025, declining by as much as 7-8% in March and May this year compared to 2024, with tourists from Canada and South Korea showing the biggest decreases (-16.8% and -11.3%, respectively).

This is corroborated by Booking’s 2Q 2025 earnings report, which showed that US room nights grew most slowly amongst all its regions:

Source: Booking Q2 2025 earnings presentation

Even though Booking does not disclose the number of listed properties by geography, the strength in Booking’s room nights for the first two quarters (up 7% and 8% respectively) highlights that the company is well-diversified and not heavily dependent on the number of inbound US visitors.

The “Connected Trip” vision for the future

In 2019, Booking CEO Glenn Fogel laid out a vision of the “Connected Trip”, in which an AI-enabled virtual travel agent will help customers seamlessly plan and book all aspects of a fully personalized trip, including home to airport transportation, flights, car rentals, hotels, restaurants, tours, and tickets to local events & attractions. The virtual agent will also be able to take action to help handle exceptions, such as re-booking hotels in the event a customer misses connecting flights, or re-booking or suggesting alternate restaurants should the customer miss a dining reservation.

Some competing travel websites, such as TripAdvisor, have created generative AI tools to suggest ideas and itineraries; most are still in their infancy stages, and none have come close to what Booking has envisioned.

As a frequent traveler, I have found the trip planning to be a highly time consuming and tedious pain point. In addition to charting out my route through unfamiliar places in strange countries, I need to connect all my stops with the right modes of transportation (which is not always obvious), punctuate each stop with an accommodation that strikes a balance between location, safety, quality, & budget, and plan my accommodations that are within reasonable travel distance from the activities I wish to partake in.

When fully implemented, the Connected Trip vision will solve this pain point. It will transform Booking from a reservation website that offers customers individual components of a trip (e.g., flights, hotels, transportation, attractions) into an integrated one-stop-shop for travel needs. Not only will Booking capture more of a traveler’s budget, but it will also gain more bargaining power over its suppliers, help protect travelers from trip disruptions as they go through their journey, and create new sources of revenue and cost savings that we cannot even imagine today.

Even though the ultimate vision will take years to fulfill, management has reported progress in cross-selling flights and ground transportation in addition to accommodations. Personally, I have responded to Booking’s nudges, as I find it far more convenient to have my hotel and flight reservations in one place (particularly with airlines that I do not have frequent flier status with) so I do not fumble around with multiple itineraries in crowded airports.

Concerns

Competition

Google Gemini, which seamlessly accesses Google Maps, Google Reviews, and Google Flights, has been a surprisingly capable travel assistant. With proper prompting (e.g., “Plan me a 7-day drive from Bozeman, Montana, to Boise, Idaho. Where possible, route me through scenic areas and Lewis and Clark points of interest. Provide commentary on things to look out for. Limit daily drives to 4 hours. Suggest mid-range hotels at each stop that allow pets, and local restaurants and their recommended dishes”), Gemini can already generate a detailed itinerary that reasonably demanding travelers can use as a basis to make reservations and other arrangements.

Other travel websites or new startups could develop AI agents that can make reservations for customers, potentially leapfrogging Booking in its Connected Trip initiative. However, as the leading OTA, Booking’s large trove of customer data gives it a significant advantage to train its AI models and agents.

Furthermore, Booking’s reputation for dependability and superb execution creates stickiness, particularly for customers traveling to unfamiliar places.

Reduced travel due to recessions or another pandemic

We have been through sharp recessions and the COVID-19 pandemic in recent years, during which Booking’s stock price have experienced sharp drawdowns. However, I believe OTA industry leaders like Booking will likely gain share in tough economic conditions and emerge stronger as smaller OTAs and mom & pop travel agents that do not have the financial stability go out of business.

Valuation

With a PE of almost 38x, Booking has the highest valuation (orange line).

However, this is partially justified by the fact that its revenue has grown faster than both Airbnb and Expedia (purple and blue lines).

In addition, the company is highly cash generative, with a trailing twelve-month (TTM) free cash flow yield (after credit losses & chargebacks and stock-based compensation) of about 4.5%, which I believe is not an overly excessive valuation.

Summary

Booking continues to widen its lead over its competitors due to powerful network effects and operating leverage and is growing briskly by expanding its market share in the alternative accommodation segment.

Unlike some of its competitors, Booking is hedged against recent government crackdowns on alternative accommodations in some cities, and its strong international presence insulates it from short term changes in travel patterns.

If successful, Booking’s Connected Trip product has the potential to transform the travel planning landscape and capture more wallet share from more travelers.

At current levels, its valuation is fair but not cheap. I am holding onto my position and plan to add on pullbacks

data.title: Moderna Vs BioNTech: Who's Winning The Post-COVID Race
data.description:
And so, I've reached the publication of the sixth article, following "Pfizer vs. Johnson & Johnson: Who's the Better Bargain for Investors" and "Eli Lilly vs. Novo Nordisk: Which Is the Better Bargain for Investors," in a series of comparison analyses of two pharmaceutical stocks.

In today's article, I'll choose which of BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX) and Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA) is more attractive after the end of the COVID-19 era, whose pipeline of product candidates is more promising in the fight against cancer, and which of these two is more 'protected' from the attacks of President Trump and RFK Jr. on the vaccine industry.

So, let's get started.

Current Valuation of BioNTech vs. Moderna

As in my last comparison article, "Teva vs. Viatris: Who Will Dominate in President Trump's America," below you will see a table with the values of each of the parameters by which I will compare the two companies.

Further, I'll present a detailed analysis of those that, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, carry the most weight in determining whether BioNTech or Moderna is more undervalued within the "Current Valuation" category.

So, I'll start by comparing how Moderna and BioNTech's revenues changed after the COVID-19 pandemic ended and Robert F Kennedy Jr. took over as Secretary of HHS on February 13, 2025, an event that, in my view, led to a more restrictive vaccine policy against this virus.

Next, I'll determine how often these two stocks beat Wall Street analysts' expectations and only then compare their price-to-sales [P/S] ratios over the past 12 months.

Moderna's revenue was $142 million in Q2 2025, up 31.5% quarter-on-quarter but down 41.1% year-on-year.

Consequently, its P/S ratio [TTM] is 3.45x, 12.74% lower than the sector median and 85.6% lower than its 5-year average.

If I look at how many times in the last 12 quarters Moderna has beaten the consensus revenue estimate, the number is 10.

With BioNTech, the situation is a little different, it seems to me, for the obvious reason that its partner in the Comirnaty franchise, namely Pfizer (PFE), has more experience and a marketing budget to minimize the current situation.

What am I talking about?

By this 'situation', I mean that after the completion of the stimulus programs promoted by the White House from 2021 to 2023, people are becoming less eager to get vaccinated with the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines.

So, BioNTech's revenue was not only bigger than Moderna's, reaching about $301.6 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, but its growth rate was also higher, namely 113.9% year-on-year and 45.8% quarter-on-quarter.

Its P/S ratio [TTM] is 7.42x, which is 87.4% higher than the sector median.

Thus, I can only conclude that Moderna is significantly more undervalued by this metric than BioNTech.

How often has BioNTech beaten analysts' expectations?

It has beaten Wall Street's revenue estimates six times out of the last 12 quarters, four quarters fewer than Moderna.

Now I'll move on to discussing the balance sheets of both companies.

According to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, analyzing total debt, the total debt/EBITDA ratio, and the maturity dates of Moderna/BioNTech's notes is crucial, and I will explain why later.

Does BioNTech or Moderna have lower total debt and total debt/EBITDA ratio?

Moderna's total debt was $741 million at the end of Q2 2025, down $528 million over the past 12 months.

Meanwhile, its competitor's total debt in the vaccine market has fluctuated around $260.4 million over the past 10 quarters, reaching $317.6 million at the end of June.

Let's continue.

Since the operating income and EBITDA of Moderna and BioNTech are negative, I believe that instead of discussing their total debt/EBITDA ratios, it would be more appropriate to compare how much cash is on their balance sheets.

BioNTech's cash and short-term investments amount to about $16.5 billion, compared to Moderna's $5.1 billion.

Also, if I look at how it's declined year-on-year, BioNTech again emerges as the ‘winner’ [-$2 billion vs. -$3.36 billion in the case of Moderna].

Thus, I conclude that BioNTech has greater financial flexibility and, more importantly, its balance sheet allows it to be more aggressive in R&D.

I'm not even taking into account that it also has strong partnership agreements with Pfizer and, especially, Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) [up to $11.1 billion] .

I will discuss the deal between BioNTech and Bristol-Myers Squibb in the next section.

Since Moderna and BioNTech don't pay dividends, it's logical that I move on to the final question.

Who won in the "Current Valuation" category?

To answer this question, I created a heat map that shows the winner more clearly than a table.

As a result, BioNTech won in the "Current Valuation" category with 4 points, beating Moderna by 2 points.

Portfolio of drugs and product candidates of Moderna vs. BioNTech

In this category, my main goal is to determine whether Moderna or BioNTech has a better chance of catching up to the leaders in the vaccine and oncology treatment markets, such as Merck (MRK), Novartis (NVS), and Sanofi (SNY).

To choose the 'winner', I ask the first question.

Does BioNTech or Moderna have a more promising portfolio of approved vaccines?

Moderna's portfolio consists of Spikevax, mNEXSPIKE, an mRNA vaccine with one-fifth the dose of Spikevax, and mRESVIA, an RSV [respiratory syncytial virus] vaccine.

And it would seem that "more means better," but I believe it's not.

mRESVIA was first approved by the FDA on May 31, 2024, to protect adults over 60 years of age from lower respiratory tract illness caused by RSV, and its label was expanded on June 12, 2025, to also include those 18-59 years of age.

But when I opened Moderna's latest Form 10-Qs/10-K, I saw that its sales are not only negligible but almost zero.

I think the two key reasons for this current situation are people's distrust of mRNA vaccines after the 'COVID-19 era', and the fact that Pfizer's Abrysvo and GSK's Arexvy (GSK) remain the preferred choices of doctors.

Overall, sales of these two vaccines [$143 million vs. $88 million] are as follows.

Ultimately, I believe that weak sales of Moderna's three vaccines, as well as the lack of a partner like Pfizer that could share operating expenses, have hit its EBIT hard.

How hard?

Moderna's EBIT was -$907 million in Q2 2025, compared to -$514.4 million for BioNTech.

Who has a more promising portfolio of product candidates? Moderna or BioNTech?

In my opinion, the situation here is also ambiguous.

Moderna's portfolio is richer in experimental vaccines targeting a wide range of viruses, including HSV [mRNA-1608], Epstein-Barr virus [mRNA-1189 and mRNA-1195], and norovirus [mRNA-1403 and mRNA-1405].

But of course, its 'crown jewel' is mRNA-4157, an mRNA vaccine developed jointly with Merck to combat renal cell carcinoma, melanoma, bladder cancer, and non-small cell lung cancer.

While BioNTech is more focused on developing bispecific antibodies such as BNT327, BNT312 [in partnership with Genmab] (GMAB), and BNT314, as well as so-called ADCs [antibody-drug conjugates] against breast cancer and solid tumors.

So, I want to clarify here that BNT327 [BMS986545] is an anti-PD-L1/VEGF-A bispecific antibody.

On June 2, 2025, Bristol-Myers Squibb announced that it had entered into a deal with BioNTech to develop this product candidate, which, to my great surprise, is being developed for 20 indications as monotherapy and in combination with trastuzumab pamirtecan and BNT325.

BioNTech's R&D portfolio includes 50 projects, six of which are in Phase III. Moderna has 35 clinical-stage projects, seven of which are in pivotal clinical trials.

Who won in the "Approved Drugs and Pipeline Candidates" category?

Thus, BioNTech became the winner, scoring 7 points.

Technical analysis and Wall Street Ratings of Moderna stock vs. BioNTech stock

So, according to the parameter values that I have noted in the table below, Moderna is a more attractive stock if I take technical analysis into account only.

I want to start with a 1-M timeframe.

Moderna stock shows more signs of being 'oversold' than BioNTech stock.

So, the price of Moderna shares remains slightly above the first standard deviation band formed from the VWAP [-1σ], and its RSI [33.28] has been near the oversold zone since November 1, 2024.

While BioNTech shares remain near VWAP [$103.60], and according to my assessment, it is completing wave C as part of the "zigzag" corrective pattern. If I switch to the 1-W timeframe, I believe that its stock price may correct further until it reaches a strong support zone lying between $95-$95.70.

Does Moderna or BioNTech have a greater upside relative to the average price target?

Once again, I cannot do without the data provided by Seeking Alpha.

According to it, the average PT [price target] for BioNTech is $134.76, indicating a potential upside of about 31.73% from the current share price [$104.90]. Meanwhile, if I turn to Moderna, the PT for it is $44.30, implying an upside of 65.67%.

Who won in the "Technical Analysis and Wall Street Ratings" category?

Moderna won this category with 4 points, beating BioNTech by 3.5 points.

But I can't stop at technical analysis alone, so let's continue.

Long-term prospects of BioNTech vs. Moderna

What do I expect from Moderna in the long term?

I expect Moderna's non-GAAP earnings per share to grow to $3.20 in 2029.

This figure is slightly above the median of a broad range of Wall Street analyst expectations [from -$3.41 to $20.62] because I am more optimistic about mNEXSPIKE sales and also believe mRNA-4157 will be a 'blockbuster' in the treatment of melanoma.

What do I expect from BioNTech in the long term?

I estimate that BioNTech's non-GAAP EPS and revenue growth will be higher than Moderna's over the next five years, reaching $2.35 and $4.4 billion, respectively.

These figures are at the upper end of the analyst ranges [from -$7.75 to $3.42 and from $2.05 billion to $4.44 billion] due to my more optimistic expectations for Comirnaty sales, as well as the launches of BNT327 and trastuzumab pamirtecan.

Who won in the "Long-Term Prospects" category?

Moderna beat BioNTech by 7 points.

Takeaway

So, I've reached the final part of the article.

Who is the winner?

But before I answer this question, I want to note that I am starting to cover Moderna and BioNTech with a 'Buy' rating.

However, because BioNTech scored 0.7 points higher, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, it's a more promising stock than Moderna.
